coredata.dc:title: Substitutional impurity in the graphene quantum dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Physica E: Low-dimensional Systems and Nanostructures
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Sierański, K.
Szatkowski, J.
coredata.dc:description: The process of formation of the localized defect states due to substitutional impurity in sp 2-bonded graphene quantum dot is considered using a simple tight-binding-type calculation. We took into account the interaction of the quantum dot atoms surrounding the substitutional impurity from the second row of elements. To saturate the external dangling sp 2 orbitals of the carbon additionally 18 hydrogen atoms were introduced. The chemical formula of the quantum dot is H18C51X, where X is the symbol of substitutional atom. The position of the localized levels is determined relative to the host-atoms (C) εp energies. We focused on the effect of substitutional doping by the B, N and O on the eigenstate energies and on the total energy change of the graphene dots including for O the effect of lattice distorsion. We conclude that B, N, and O can form stable substitutional defects in graphene quantum dot.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene
Quantum dots
Defects
Tight-binding model

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Red – the sp 2 orbitals of the substational impurity, black – the 51 carbon host atoms surrounding the impurity, blue – 18 surrounding hydrogen atoms.

Schematic illustration of geometric settings for (a) sp 2 orbitals of the substational atom or host (C) atom (1′, 2′, 3′) relative to the surrounding C atoms (1″, 2″, 3″), (b) s orbital of hydrogen (H) atom (0) relative to the nearest sp 2 orbitals of the C atom (1, 2, 3).
The eigenstate energies of the dot without the substitutional impurity. For pz states the index of eigenstates are increased by 61.

The eigenstate energies for p
z of the dot with the substational impurities: (a) oxygen, (b) boron, and (c) nitrogen. The two states at the Fermi level (zero energy) are occupied: for B with one electron, for C with two and for N with three electrons. For O two states are entirely occupied by four electrons and the lowest unoccupied state (LUMO) is 1.36eV above the Fermi level.
The eigenstate energies of the dot with the substational O impurity. The arrow indicates the lowest positive energy level sp 2. For pz states the index of eigenstates are increased by 61.

The s and p atomic orbital energies (in eV) as well as the the hybrid atomic energy
ε
h
( κ )
and the dehybridization energy
Δ
( κ )
of the substitutional and the host atoms used in calculations.
The total electron energies (in eV) of the dot without and with the substational impurity.

row.entry: Atom in the center of dot
Difference between the total energy of the ideal dot and the dot with vacancy in the middle of the dot

head.ce:title: Substitutional impurity in the graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Fundamental Problems of Technology, Wroclaw University of Technology, Wybrzeże Wyspiańskiego 27, 50-370 Wrocław, Poland

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Fundamental Problems of Technology, Wroclaw University of Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The process of formation of the localized defect states due to substitutional impurity in sp 2-bonded graphene quantum dot is considered using a simple tight-binding-type calculation. We took into account the interaction of the quantum dot atoms surrounding the substitutional impurity from the second row of elements. To saturate the external dangling sp 2 orbitals of the carbon additionally 18 hydrogen atoms were introduced. The chemical formula of the quantum dot is H18C51X, where X is the symbol of substitutional atom. The position of the localized levels is determined relative to the host-atoms (C) εp energies. We focused on the effect of substitutional doping by the B, N and O on the eigenstate energies and on the total energy change of the graphene dots including for O the effect of lattice distorsion. We conclude that B, N, and O can form stable substitutional defects in graphene quantum dot.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene, just one layer of carbon atoms, is an isolated graphite layer. It is the first example of truly two dimensional crystals Carbon atoms arranged in a two-dimensional honeycomb lattice form a building block for three dimensional graphite, as well as “one-dimensional” carbon nanotubes and “zero-dimensional” fullerenes. It is a gapless semiconductor with unique electronic properties resulting from a charge-conjugation symmetry between electrons and holes and it possesses an internal degree of freedom similar to a “chirality” for ultrarelativistic elementary particles. The high electron mobility in graphene and its unique electrical properties make it a prospective material for next-generation, carbon-based electronics. The prospects for future application in nanoelectronics have put graphene in the focus of many theoretical and experimental studies (see Ref. [1] and references therein).

Following the progress in the fabrication of graphene based devices [1], lower dimensional structures such as graphene ribbons [2,3], and more recently graphene quantum dots [4], are attracting increasing attention due to their nontrivial electronic and magnetic properties. In particular, it was shown that when an electron is confined to a triangular atomic thick layer of graphene with zigzag edges, its energy spectrum collapses to a shell of degenerate states at the Fermi level (Dirac point) [5] similar to the edge states in graphene ribbons [2,3], but isolated from the remaining states by a gap. This opens up the possibility to design a strongly correlated electronic system as a function of fractional filling of the shell, in analogy to the fractional quantum Hall effect [6], but without the need for a magnetic field.

Applications exploiting graphene's unique electronic properties require an understanding of impurity effects in this material. Absorbates on graphene as well as the intentionally introduced impurities are sources of a free electron or a hole and they create scattering centers. Among the scattering mechanisms limiting the electron mobility in graphene under discussion there are charged impurities [7] and resonant states related to adsorbates on graphene (see Refs. [8–13] and references therein). Wehling et al. [13] discussed the influence of adsorbates on the electrical properties of graphene. According to this survey open-shell adsorbates like NO2, alkali and halogen adatoms (except for fluorine) are very reactive and they act as direct dopants. They accept or donate one electron and bind ionically to graphene but they hybridize weakly with the graphene bands and they experience very low migration barriers. Recent theoretical and experimental studies on graphene doping showed the possibility of substituting C atoms with B and N atoms [14–16]. Substitutional doping can modify the electronic band structure of graphene.

In Ref. [14] the authors reported on doping graphene by substituting some C atoms with N atoms. N-doped graphene was obtained by NH3 annealing after N+-ion irradiation of graphene samples. The authors observed a transition of graphene from p-type to n-type after an annealing of irradiated samples in NH3. In Ref. [15] the authors reported that substitutional doping during growth could be used to alter the electronic properties of monolayer graphene. They used scanning tunneling microscopy, Raman spectroscopy, X-ray spectroscopy, and first-principles calculations to characterize individual nitrogen dopants in the monolayer graphene grown on a copper substrate. They found that nitrogen atoms occupied the place of carbon atoms, and that the extra electron introduced together with the nitrogen changed properties of the electronic structure of graphene, but only in the range of a few lattice spacing of the nitrogen dopant site. In Ref. [16] the authors performed an ab initio total-energy calculations to investigate the effect of substitutional B and N doping in the electronic and structural properties of graphene with grain-boundary defects. Their results indicated that the incorporation of N and B atoms into the defective region was an energetically favorable process. The calculated electronic properties showed that the incorporation of a single substitutional B (N) atom produced p-type (n-type) doping in all investigated grain-boundary structures.

In our previous paper [17] we have presented the results of calculations of the energy levels introduced by doping of graphene layers by substitutional impurities of the second period of the periodic table using the Green's-functions framework and the tight-binding method. We discussed the possibility of the existence of well-defined resonant states that coincide with the valence band and the conduction band near the Dirac point. We concluded that in the case of a high substitutional doping by O the Fermi level could be attached to the appropriate energy level and the resonant motion of the conduction electrons may be significantly disrupted.

In this paper we present the results of energy-level calculations associated with the substitutional impurities in graphene quantum dots. The ideal unrelaxed substitutional impurities are defined by replacing carbon atom at the center of a quantum dots by an impurity atom and considering the positions of all other atoms to be unchanged. According to that simple model we discuss the effect of substitutional doping by B, N, and O on the total energy change of the graphene dots, as well as the energy levels introduced by the dopants.

For O we examined the effect of lattice distorsion resulting from replacement of the central carbon atom by O atom. In our calculations we assumed that the distortion is a function of the atomic radius.

We consider two dimensional honeycomb lattice composed of N (N=51) carbon atoms placed at the positions
r →
i
(i=1, 2, N) of two different kinds of atoms (κ=κ(i)=1,2) and an substitutional impurity atom placed at
r →
0
position and an M=18 hydrogen atoms which task is to saturate dangling sp 2 orbitals of the outside carbon atoms (see Fig. 1). We consider the both the unrelaxed and relaxed lattice around the substitutional impurity by considering the positions of the atoms surrounding the impurity are unchanged and changed, respectively.

The calculations were carried out using tight-binding method. As a basis for the expansion of electron wave function of substitutional and host atoms we used p
z atomic orbitals and sp 2 hybrids. For the atom at the positions

energy of dehybridization (metal binding, promotion energy). The values of s and p atomic energies for host (carbon) and substitutional atoms from the second row of elements were taken from the work of Herman and Skillman [18] and are presented in Table 1.

In the base of sp 2 hybrids the interactions between the substitutional and the nearest-neighbor host atoms as between the all carbon atoms in the graphene dot are described by four matrix elements β 1 to the β 4.

According to Fig. 2 they are

At first we have taken the assumption that the perturbation due to a substitutional atom is localized on defect site. Thus, the values of the on-site matrix elements on all host atoms are not affected by the present of the defect. The all (host–host and host-defect) of-site matrix elements were considered within the nearest-neighbor and two-center approximation. That means that coupling of the substitutional atom to neighbors is described by the transfer matrix elements vanishing except those between the interstitial atom and its six nearest neighbors. The nearest-neighbor transfer-matrix elements are found to scale as the inverse square of the bond length d [19]. For example for the unrelaxed host (C) lattice theirs values have been:
V
s s σ
= − 5.27
eV
,
V
p p σ
= 12.22
eV
,
V
p p π
= − 3.05
eV
,
V
s p σ
= 6.94
eV
. The second-nearest and more-distant transfer-matrix elements were neglected. Substituting the parameters in β 1 to the β 4 we obtain: β 1=−16.45eV, β 2=−0.68eV, β 3=−2.05eV, β 4=1.00eV. Note that the value of matrix element like β 1 is more than 8 times bigger than β 3 and β 4 and about 25 times bigger than β 2. In further calculations we will ignore β 2 assuming that |β 1|»|β 2|.

In the base of sp 2 hybrids of carbon atoms and the interactions s orbital of surrounding the graphene dot hydrogen atoms are described by two matrix elements

(see Fig. 2). In terms of V 11 ,m interactions and using the value of atomic hydrogen–carbon distance as

they are: β 1=−11.47eV and α 2=0.28eV. As α1 and more than 40 times bigger than α 2 in further calculations we will ignore α 2 assuming that |α 1|»|α 2|.

For the relaxed lattice of graphene dot caused by putting in place of the host atom the substitutional impurity the nearest-neighbor transfer-matrix elements are also taken to scale as the inverse square of the bond length which for the impurity and the first three atoms surrounding the impurity
d 2
=
d
C − C
/ 2 +
a I
= 1.24
A ∘
, where a
I is the ionic radius. The distance between the first three carbon atoms surrounding the impurity and the six more distant atoms are calculated assuming that they remain at their position after putting in place of the host atom the substitutional impurity [see Fig. 2].

Fig. 3 shows the energy spectrum of the dot without the substitutional impurity. Similarly, as for trapezoidal graphene structure with 38 atoms 6 we have two degenerate states at the Fermi level (Dirac point) isolated from the remaining states by a gap. The total electron energy of the dot is equal to −3518.2eV.

Now consider the influence of the substitution of the central C atom by the N, B or O atom on the eigenstate energies. At first, let us examine the energy shift of states near the Fermi energy which are derived from atomic p
z states.

As it can be seen in Fig. 4 qualitatively, the system states are not significantly affected. At the Fermi level, we have two states, which are separated by gap from energy states lying above and below Fermi level. The highest states below the Fermi level have the energies from −1.283eV for O to −1.277eV for B. The more visible difference is for the lowest unoccupied state above the Fermi energy. In the case of B, C and N, these energies are 1.653eV, 1.651eV and 1.611eV, respectively. The two states at the Fermi level (zero energy) are occupied: for B with one electron, for C with two and for N with three electrons. For O two states are entirely occupied by four electrons and the lowest unoccupied state is 1.36eV above the Fermi level.

Replacing the central carbon atom by a substitutional atom can result in the change of the total spin of the quantum dot. For a pure graphene discussed in this paper there are 27 spins “up” and 25 “down”, so the resultant spin S=1. Thus, this type of quantum dot will exhibit a weak magnetic properties. Taking into account the disorder it is probably that twofold degenerate state at zero energy will be split to two and then S=0. For the nitrogen and boron dopant S=1/2, both with and without taking into account the disorder. Similarly, the introduction of oxygen impurities gives S=0. It should be noted that injection of one extra electron to the oxygen doped dot puts the one electron in the upper band of states pz . Magnetic properties of triangular graphene quantum dots containing more carbon atoms are analyzed in Ref. [20]. According to tight binding calculations 20 the neutral dot with 97 carbon atom and 7 electrons occupying the sevenfold degenerate states at zero energy is maximally spin polarized, with the total spin S=3.5. Such a dot has therefore a strong ferromagnetism properties.

For dot states that are derived from atomic hybridized sp 2 states the effect of replacing the central C atom by the N, B and O atom on the quantum dot eigenstate energies is qualitatively similar. The highest sp 2 states below the Fermi level have the energies of −13.504eV for O to −13.481eV for B. Similarly to pz the states, more visible difference is for the lowest unoccupied sp 2 states above the Fermi energy. In the case of B, C and N, these energies are 6.189eV, 6.187eV and 6.157eV, respectively. In the case of O the energy of the lowest unoccupied sp 2 state is equal to 4.567eV. The optical dipole transition of an electron from the Fermi level to that level is allowed and corresponding to the wavelength of 0.27μm.
The eigenstate energies of the unrelaxed (ideal) lattice dot with the substitutional O impurity are presented in Fig. 5. The arrow indicates the lowest positive energy level.

In the case of unrelaxed lattice with O atom in the center of the dot, the total electron energy of the dot is equal to −3550.8eV, so the promotion energy understood as the difference between the total energy of the dot with and without of the substitutional impurity is equal to −17.9eV.

In the Table 2 the calculated total electron energies of the dot with the substitutional impurity are presented. These energies were compared to the total electron energy of dot with atom C in the center of the quantum dot and energy of the quantum dot with vacancy in the place of central atom plus the energies of valence electrons in an isolated atom of dopant. In the case of substitutional atom in the center of quantum dot.

the lattice distortion should be taken into account. The distortion should be a function of the atomic radius, which is: 88, 70, 111, and 66pm for B, N, Be, and O, respectively. For C the atomic radius is 77pm Thus, the greatest distortion is expected for Be (excluded from our calculations), the smallest for N. We have also calculated the total energy for O assuming that the distortion lattice around the dopant through modifying only parameters β 1 and Vppπ according to the formulas β 1=β 01 (r 0/r)2 and Vppπ =V 0ppπ (r 0/r)2, where r 0 mark C ionic radius and r ionic radius of the corresponding atom impurities [10]. The results are presented in the last column of the Table 2.

We discuss the effect of substitutional doping by the B, N and O on the eigenstate energies and on the total energy change of the graphene dots. For O the effect of lattice distortion was included. According to our model B, N, and O can form stable substitutional defects, especially for O.
